topic: New topic: sharp identified regions for interval regression with several interval-measured regressors under coordinatewise or selected-coordinate monotonicity. Pre-anchor check: closest published anchor is Manski-Tamer interval regression for one interval-measured scalar regressor, with later set-inference work for the resulting identified set. Our theorem is not that because multi-coordinate interval regressors create a partial order: worst-case completions are governed by antichain/frontier geometry rather than the one-dimensional lower/upper endpoint ordering. Require a concrete nonroutine object: an antichain certificate or minimal frontier basis for sharp lower and upper regression-function envelopes, plus a finite two-regressor witness where coordinatewise monotonicity yields a nonrectangular identified region that cannot be recovered by intersecting one-dimensional Manski-Tamer bounds. If the proposal reduces to generic LP duality, ordinary interval arithmetic, or a definition-unfold frontier, pivot or stop early.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp lower and upper regression-function envelopes for several interval-measured regressors under full coordinatewise monotonicity using a minimal antichain or frontier basis.

source_refs.claim: Manski and Tamer study interval regression when interval data are available on one variable and maintain interval, monotonicity, and mean-independence assumptions to obtain simple nonparametric bounds.
source_refs.author: Charles F. Manski and Elie Tamer

source_refs.claim: Beresteanu, Molchanov, and Molinari cover interval outcome and covariate data through set-valued predictions, Aumann expectations, support functions, and convex programming rather than scalar endpoint ordering.
source_refs.author: Arie Beresteanu, Ilya Molchanov, and Francesca Molinari

source_refs.excerpt: latent integrable scalar regressor v, and observed integrable scalar endpoints v0 and v1
lies between the supremum ... with v1 <= V and the infimum ... with v0 >= V

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published and prior CausalSmith Manski-Tamer anchors are scalar: the lower-side and upper-side classes are ordered by one-dimensional endpoint comparisons against a scalar target. General random-set and convex-moment frameworks acknowledge interval covariate data, but they route the problem through Aumann expectations or support functions and do not identify the finite frontier of incomparable endpoint cells that determines the sharp monotone envelope in a product order.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to apply the Manski-Tamer lower/upper endpoint rule coordinate by coordinate fails because Manski and Tamer explicitly study one interval-measured variable while product intervals contain incomparable endpoint cells not ordered by scalar inequalities b <= V and a >= V.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a sharp identification theorem giving lower and upper conditional-mean envelopes as extrema over a minimal antichain/frontier certificate, with necessity and sufficiency for sharpness.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize sharp identified regions when monotonicity is imposed only on selected coordinates of several interval-measured regressors and the remaining interval coordinates are unrestricted.

source_refs.claim: The IMMI analysis imposes monotonicity in the scalar interval-measured regressor and no restrictions on the distribution of unobserved values inside the observed intervals.
source_refs.author: Charles F. Manski and Elie Tamer

source_refs.claim: Magnac and Maurin show that additional information on the within-interval distribution of an interval-measured latent regressor shrinks the identified set, with point identification only under uniformity within intervals.
source_refs.author: Thierry Magnac and Eric Maurin

source_refs.claim: Manski and Pepper show that monotone restrictions can sharpen partial-identification bounds, alone and in combination with other monotone response restrictions, but in scalar ordered-instrument settings.
source_refs.author: Charles F. Manski and John V. Pepper

source_refs.excerpt: The Monotonicity assumption is that the conditional mean function m_x(t) is weakly increasing in t
T-3c ... requires `X0 subset XA0 intersect XB0`

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing monotone-restriction anchors sharpen bounds in scalar or fully ordered settings, while the accepted nonparametric-bounds artifact shows that intersections need explicit containment hypotheses. With selected-coordinate monotonicity, unrestricted interval coordinates remain part of the endpoint cell and can change feasible latent placements; a projection or marginalization argument is therefore not justified by the scalar IMMI proof.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to project away unrestricted interval coordinates fails because the Manski-Tamer proof conditions on full endpoint cells and Magnac-Maurin show that within-interval distributional information changes the identified set.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a necessary-and-sufficient sharp-bound theorem separating selected monotone coordinates from unrestricted interval fibers and stating the frontier or measurable-selection conditions for attainability.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove a sharp latent-completion theorem for multivariate interval regression under coordinatewise monotone mean independence.

source_refs.claim: Beresteanu and Molinari use set-valued random variables and Aumann expectations for partially identified regions, with interval-identified scalar parameters and interval-data examples, while noting nonconvex cases are handled through convex hulls.
source_refs.author: Arie Beresteanu and Francesca Molinari

source_refs.claim: Beresteanu, Molchanov, and Molinari emphasize that different econometric frameworks require different random-set constructions for the observationally equivalent random elements.
source_refs.author: Arie Beresteanu, Ilya Molchanov, and Francesca Molinari

source_refs.excerpt: intervals crossing V can be completed by choosing the latent conditional distribution inside the interval

open_problems.why_unsolved: The scalar proof sketch asserts sharpness by completing crossing intervals, and the pipeline explicitly flags sharpness via measurable selection as a missing architecture front. Random-set papers provide general tools for set-valued selections, but the multivariate monotone case must construct one joint latent completion that respects a partial order across observed product intervals, not independent scalar completions cell by cell.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to paste scalar endpoint mixtures coordinate by coordinate fails because the pipeline flags sharpness via measurable selection as missing and the multivariate completion must preserve one joint coordinatewise monotonicity restriction over a partial order.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a measurable-selection or finite-support completion theorem showing that every point in the proposed multivariate envelope is observationally attainable under the interval, monotonicity, and mean-independence restrictions.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct a finite two-regressor witness where coordinatewise monotonicity yields a nonrectangular identified region not recoverable by intersecting one-dimensional Manski-Tamer bounds.

source_refs.claim: Manski and Tamer obtain simple scalar nonparametric interval-regression bounds under interval, monotonicity, and mean-independence assumptions.
source_refs.author: Charles F. Manski and Elie Tamer

source_refs.claim: Chernozhukov, Lee, and Rosen formulate many sharp bounds as intersection bounds or infinite-constraint programs, warning that simple analog intersection estimators can understate identified sets.
source_refs.author: Victor Chernozhukov, Sokbae Lee, and Adam M. Rosen

source_refs.excerpt: generically nonrectangular: it is a proper subset of the product
failure of a coordinatewise rectangular relaxation is useful

open_problems.why_unsolved: Published scalar interval-regression bounds are marginal envelope statements, while adjacent CausalSmith artifacts identify nonrectangularity as the real novelty when shared latent coordinates make separate projections too wide. The frontdoor reviewer also warns that a single rectangularization witness is not enough unless the observable law and sharpness construction are explicit; this topic needs the analogous witness for interval-measured regressors and monotone completions.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to intersect one-dimensional Manski-Tamer bounds fails because prior nonrectangularity artifacts show that shared latent coordinates can make the product of separately feasible projections too wide.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a finite two-regressor observed law with explicit coordinatewise monotone completions and two support directions proving strict containment relative to the rectangular intersection of one-dimensional bounds.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop consistency or confidence-region theory for plug-in estimators of multivariate interval-regression frontier envelopes when the active antichain or frontier is estimated.

source_refs.claim: Chernozhukov, Hong, and Tamer develop estimation and confidence regions for partially identified models characterized by moment inequalities and equalities, including regressions with missing and corrupted data.
source_refs.author: Victor Chernozhukov, Han Hong, and Elie Tamer

source_refs.claim: Intersection-bound analog estimators can be severely finite-sample biased and tend to underestimate set size, motivating specialized bias correction and inference.
source_refs.author: Victor Chernozhukov, Sokbae Lee, and Adam M. Rosen

source_refs.excerpt: sample index classes are fixed or converge in the standard way needed for taking suprema and infima
set convergence - three research-level econometric formalization fronts

open_problems.why_unsolved: The scalar artifact assumes uniform convergence over lower-side and upper-side classes that are fixed or converge in a standard way, and the pipeline separately flags set-valued estimator consistency as a research-level front. General moment-inequality and intersection-bound inference papers help after a region is specified, but a multivariate frontier estimator changes the active antichain that defines the endpoint functional.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to reuse scalar uniform-convergence bounds fails because the scalar source assumes fixed or standard-convergent lower/upper index classes, whereas a multivariate frontier estimator changes the optimizing antichain class.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A resolution would be a set-estimator consistency theorem or endpoint confidence-region construction for support-function/frontier functionals indexed by estimated antichain classes.

literature_map:
Manski and Tamer (2002, Econometrica) is the closest anchor: interval data on one variable, scalar monotonicity, mean independence, and simple nonparametric bounds.
The topic is not resolved there because the paper explicitly works with one interval-measured regressor or outcome while other variables are precisely measured.
Manski and Pepper (2000, Econometrica) shows how weak monotone inequalities can sharpen partial-identification bounds, but it is not a multi-interval-regressor frontier result.
Magnac and Maurin (2008, ReStud) studies monotone binary models with interval-measured latent regressors and shows within-interval distributional information shrinks identified sets.
Beresteanu and Molinari (2008, Econometrica) gives inference for partially identified regions represented through set-valued random variables, including interval-data examples and convex-hull caveats.
Beresteanu, Molchanov, and Molinari (2011, Econometrica) supplies a broad random-set/support-function framework for convex moment predictions, including interval covariate data, but not an explicit coordinatewise antichain envelope.
Beresteanu, Molchanov, and Molinari (2012, Journal of Econometrics) emphasizes that random-set construction depends on the econometric framework, which is relevant to multivariate latent completions.
Chernozhukov, Hong, and Tamer (2007, Econometrica) provides general parameter-set inference for moment-inequality/equality models, useful only after the identified region is characterized.
Chernozhukov, Lee, and Rosen (2013, Econometrica) is central for intersection bounds: many bounds are infimum/supremum or infinite-constraint programs, and naive analog estimators can understate set size.
The live identification gap is an antichain/frontier theorem for product-interval monotonicity plus a nonrectangular two-regressor witness; generic LP duality or ordinary interval arithmetic would not meet it.
A secondary inference gap remains for plug-in endpoint/frontier estimators once the active frontier is estimated.
The study-pipeline Manski-Tamer multivariate-regressor gap was marked all_failed, so it is treated here as a hypothesis to validate against these published anchors rather than as established prior fact.
prior_proposal_map:
Direct qid pid_multivariate_interval_regression has only state/pipeline scaffolding, with no proposal or review artifacts yet.
Highest-signal prior source is _literature_bank/_failed/architecture_missing/manski_tamer_interval_regression_v1: scalar interval-regression bounds, scalar lower/upper endpoint classes, sharpness-by-completion language, and a pipeline note flagging measurable-selection/set-convergence architecture gaps.
The study-pipeline gap oq_multiple_interval_regressors matches the topic but originated from an all_failed insight, so the final entries use it only as a hypothesis validated by papers and repo artifacts.
Accepted manski_nonparametric_bounds_v1 supplies adjacent level-set/intersection machinery and explicit containment/boundedness drift-watch issues relevant to selected-coordinate monotonicity.
pid_ordinal_principal_strata_v1 supplies a live PartialID nonrectangularity analogue: a proper-subset projection caused by shared latent coordinates.
eid_frontdoor_multi_mediator_v1 reviewer JSON supplies a cautionary adjacent diagnosis: coordinatewise rectangular-relaxation witnesses need an actual observable-law and sharpness construction, not generic LP projection alone.
pid_interval_censored_adoption_v1 was inspected as an interval-data adjacent failure, but it is panel/timing-specific and mostly useful as a warning against repackaging finite refinement bounds.
No reviewer JSON under the direct qid yielded strengthening_paths because the run has not yet produced review artifacts.
